  4â†’
  5â†’@file:JvmMultifileClass
  6â†’@file:JvmName("JobKt")
  7â†’@file:Suppress("DEPRECATION_ERROR", "RedundantUnitReturnType")
  8â†’
  9â†’package kotlinx.coroutines
 10â†’
 11â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.selects.*
 12â†’import kotlin.coroutines.*
 13â†’import kotlin.jvm.*
 14â†’
 15â†’// --------------- core job interfaces ---------------
 16â†’
 17â†’/**
 18â†’ * A background job. Conceptually, a job is a cancellable thing with a life-cycle that
 19â†’ * culminates in its completion.
 20â†’ *
 21â†’ * Jobs can be arranged into parent-child hierarchies where cancellation
 22â†’ * of a parent leads to immediate cancellation of all its [children] recursively.
 23â†’ * Failure of a child with an exception other than [CancellationException] immediately cancels its parent and,
 24â†’ * consequently, all its other children. This behavior can be customized using [SupervisorJob].
 25â†’ *
 26â†’ * The most basic instances of `Job` interface are created like this:
 27â†’ *
 28â†’ * * **Coroutine job** is created with [launch][CoroutineScope.launch] coroutine builder.
 29â†’ *   It runs a specified block of code and completes on completion of this block.
 30â†’ * * **[CompletableJob]** is created with a `Job()` factory function.
 31â†’ *   It is completed by calling [CompletableJob.complete].
 32â†’ *
 33â†’ * Conceptually, an execution of a job does not produce a result value. Jobs are launched solely for their
 34â†’ * side effects. See [Deferred] interface for a job that produces a result.
 35â†’ *
 36â†’ * ### Job states
 37â†’ *
 38â†’ * A job has the following states:
 39â†’ *
 40â†’ * | **State**                        | [isActive] | [isCompleted] | [isCancelled] |
 41â†’ * | -------------------------------- | ---------- | ------------- | ------------- |
 42â†’ * | _New_ (optional initial state)   | `false`    | `false`       | `false`       |
 43â†’ * | _Active_ (default initial state) | `true`     | `false`       | `false`       |
 44â†’ * | _Completing_ (transient state)   | `true`     | `false`       | `false`       |
 45â†’ * | _Cancelling_ (transient state)   | `false`    | `false`       | `true`        |
 46â†’ * | _Cancelled_ (final state)        | `false`    | `true`        | `true`        |
 47â†’ * | _Completed_ (final state)        | `false`    | `true`        | `false`       |
 48â†’ *
 49â†’ * Usually, a job is created in the _active_ state (it is created and started). However, coroutine builders
 50â†’ * that provide an optional `start` parameter create a coroutine in the _new_ state when this parameter is set to
 51â†’ * [CoroutineStart.LAZY]. Such a job can be made _active_ by invoking [start] or [join].
 52â†’ *
 53â†’ * A job is _active_ while the coroutine is working or until [CompletableJob] is completed,
 54â†’ * or until it fails or cancelled.
 55â†’ *
 56â†’ * Failure of an _active_ job with an exception makes it _cancelling_.
 57â†’ * A job can be cancelled at any time with [cancel] function that forces it to transition to
 58â†’ * the _cancelling_ state immediately. The job becomes _cancelled_  when it finishes executing its work and
 59â†’ * all its children complete.
 60â†’ *
 61â†’ * Completion of an _active_ coroutine's body or a call to [CompletableJob.complete] transitions the job to
 62â†’ * the _completing_ state. It waits in the _completing_ state for all its children to complete before
 63â†’ * transitioning to the _completed_ state.
 64â†’ * Note that _completing_ state is purely internal to the job. For an outside observer a _completing_ job is still
 65â†’ * active, while internally it is waiting for its children.
 66â†’ *
 67â†’ * ```
 68â†’ *                                       wait children
 69â†’ * +-----+ start  +--------+ complete   +-------------+  finish  +-----------+
 70â†’ * | New | -----> | Active | ---------> | Completing  | -------> | Completed |
 71â†’ * +-----+        +--------+            +-------------+          +-----------+
 72â†’ *                  |  cancel / fail       |
 73â†’ *                  |     +----------------+
 74â†’ *                  |     |
 75â†’ *                  V     V
 76â†’ *              +------------+                           finish  +-----------+
 77â†’ *              | Cancelling | --------------------------------> | Cancelled |
 78â†’ *              +------------+                                   +-----------+
 79â†’ * ```
 80â†’ *
 81â†’ * A `Job` instance in the
 82â†’ * [coroutineContext](https://kotlinlang.org/api/latest/jvm/stdlib/kotlin.coroutines/coroutine-context.html)
 83â†’ * represents the coroutine itself.
 84â†’ *
 85â†’ * ### Cancellation cause
 86â†’ *
 87â†’ * A coroutine job is said to _complete exceptionally_ when its body throws an exception;
 88â†’ * a [CompletableJob] is completed exceptionally by calling [CompletableJob.completeExceptionally].
 89â†’ * An exceptionally completed job is cancelled and the corresponding exception becomes the _cancellation cause_ of the job.
 90â†’ *
 91â†’ * Normal cancellation of a job is distinguished from its failure by the type of this exception that caused its cancellation.
 92â†’ * A coroutine that threw [CancellationException] is considered to be _cancelled normally_.
 93â†’ * If a cancellation cause is a different exception type, then the job is considered to have _failed_.
 94â†’ * When a job has _failed_, then its parent gets cancelled with the exception of the same type,
 95â†’ * thus ensuring transparency in delegating parts of the job to its children.
 96â†’ *
 97â†’ * Note, that [cancel] function on a job only accepts [CancellationException] as a cancellation cause, thus
 98â†’ * calling [cancel] always results in a normal cancellation of a job, which does not lead to cancellation
 99â†’ * of its parent. This way, a parent can [cancel] its own children (cancelling all their children recursively, too)
100â†’ * without cancelling itself.
101â†’ *
102â†’ * ### Concurrency and synchronization
103â†’ *
104â†’ * All functions on this interface and on all interfaces derived from it are **thread-safe** and can
105â†’ * be safely invoked from concurrent coroutines without external synchronization.
106â†’ *
107â†’ * ### Not stable for inheritance
108â†’ *
109â†’ * **`Job` interface and all its derived interfaces are not stable for inheritance in 3rd party libraries**,
110â†’ * as new methods might be added to this interface in the future, but is stable for use.
111â†’ */
112â†’public interface Job : CoroutineContext.Element {
113â†’    /**
114â†’     * Key for [Job] instance in the coroutine context.
115â†’     */
116â†’    public companion object Key : CoroutineContext.Key<Job>
117â†’
118â†’    // ------------ state query ------------
119â†’
120â†’    /**
121â†’     * Returns the parent of the current job if the parent-child relationship
122â†’     * is established or `null` if the job has no parent or was successfully completed.
123â†’     *
124â†’     * Accesses to this property are not idempotent, the property becomes `null` as soon
125â†’     * as the job is transitioned to its final state, whether it is cancelled or completed,
126â†’     * and all job children are completed.
127â†’     *
128â†’     * For a coroutine, its corresponding job completes as soon as the coroutine itself
129â†’     * and all its children are complete.
130â†’     *
131â†’     * @see [Job] state transitions for additional details.
132â†’     */
133â†’    @ExperimentalCoroutinesApi
134â†’    public val parent: Job?
135â†’
136â†’    /**
137â†’     * Returns `true` when this job is active -- it was already started and has not completed nor was cancelled yet.
138â†’     * The job that is waiting for its [children] to complete is still considered to be active if it
139â†’     * was not cancelled nor failed.
140â†’     *
141â†’     * See [Job] documentation for more details on job states.
142â†’     */
143â†’    public val isActive: Boolean
144â†’
145â†’    /**
146â†’     * Returns `true` when this job has completed for any reason. A job that was cancelled or failed
147â†’     * and has finished its execution is also considered complete. Job becomes complete only after
148â†’     * all its [children] complete.
149â†’     *
150â†’     * See [Job] documentation for more details on job states.
151â†’     */
152â†’    public val isCompleted: Boolean
153â†’
154â†’    /**
155â†’     * Returns `true` if this job was cancelled for any reason, either by explicit invocation of [cancel] or
156â†’     * because it had failed or its child or parent was cancelled.
157â†’     * In the general case, it does not imply that the
158â†’     * job has already [completed][isCompleted], because it may still be finishing whatever it was doing and
159â†’     * waiting for its [children] to complete.
160â†’     *
161â†’     * See [Job] documentation for more details on cancellation and failures.
162â†’     */
163â†’    public val isCancelled: Boolean
164â†’
165â†’    /**
166â†’     * Returns [CancellationException] that signals the completion of this job. This function is
167â†’     * used by [cancellable][suspendCancellableCoroutine] suspending functions. They throw exception
168â†’     * returned by this function when they suspend in the context of this job and this job becomes _complete_.
169â†’     *
170â†’     * This function returns the original [cancel] cause of this job if that `cause` was an instance of
171â†’     * [CancellationException]. Otherwise (if this job was cancelled with a cause of a different type, or
172â†’     * was cancelled without a cause, or had completed normally), an instance of [CancellationException] is
173â†’     * returned. The [CancellationException.cause] of the resulting [CancellationException] references
174â†’     * the original cancellation cause that was passed to [cancel] function.
175â†’     *
176â†’     * This function throws [IllegalStateException] when invoked on a job that is still active.
177â†’     *
178â†’     * @suppress **This an internal API and should not be used from general code.**
179â†’     */
180â†’    @InternalCoroutinesApi
181â†’    public fun getCancellationException(): CancellationException
182â†’
183â†’    // ------------ state update ------------
184â†’
185â†’    /**
186â†’     * Starts coroutine related to this job (if any) if it was not started yet.
187â†’     * The result is `true` if this invocation actually started coroutine or `false`
188â†’     * if it was already started or completed.
189â†’     */
190â†’    public fun start(): Boolean
191â†’
192â†’
193â†’    /**
194â†’     * Cancels this job with an optional cancellation [cause].
195â†’     * A cause can be used to specify an error message or to provide other details on
196â†’     * the cancellation reason for debugging purposes.
197â†’     * See [Job] documentation for full explanation of cancellation machinery.
198â†’     */
199â†’    public fun cancel(cause: CancellationException? = null)
200â†’
201â†’    /**
202â†’     * @suppress This method implements old version of JVM ABI. Use [cancel].
203â†’     */
204â†’    @Deprecated(level = DeprecationLevel.HIDDEN, message = "Since 1.2.0, binary compatibility with versions <= 1.1.x")
205â†’    public fun cancel(): Unit = cancel(null)
206â†’
207â†’    /**
208â†’     * @suppress This method has bad semantics when cause is not a [CancellationException]. Use [cancel].
209â†’     */
210â†’    @Deprecated(level = DeprecationLevel.HIDDEN, message = "Since 1.2.0, binary compatibility with versions <= 1.1.x")
211â†’    public fun cancel(cause: Throwable? = null): Boolean
212â†’
213â†’    // ------------ parent-child ------------
214â†’
215â†’    /**
216â†’     * Returns a sequence of this job's children.
217â†’     *
218â†’     * A job becomes a child of this job when it is constructed with this job in its
219â†’     * [CoroutineContext] or using an explicit `parent` parameter.
220â†’     *
221â†’     * A parent-child relation has the following effect:
222â†’     *
223â†’     * * Cancellation of parent with [cancel] or its exceptional completion (failure)
224â†’     *   immediately cancels all its children.
225â†’     * * Parent cannot complete until all its children are complete. Parent waits for all its children to
226â†’     *   complete in _completing_ or _cancelling_ state.
227â†’     * * Uncaught exception in a child, by default, cancels parent. This applies even to
228â†’     *   children created with [async][CoroutineScope.async] and other future-like
229â†’     *   coroutine builders, even though their exceptions are caught and are encapsulated in their result.
230â†’     *   This default behavior can be overridden with [SupervisorJob].
231â†’     */
232â†’    public val children: Sequence<Job>
233â†’
234â†’    /**
235â†’     * Attaches child job so that this job becomes its parent and
236â†’     * returns a handle that should be used to detach it.
237â†’     *
238â†’     * A parent-child relation has the following effect:
239â†’     * * Cancellation of parent with [cancel] or its exceptional completion (failure)
240â†’     *   immediately cancels all its children.
241â†’     * * Parent cannot complete until all its children are complete. Parent waits for all its children to
242â†’     *   complete in _completing_ or _cancelling_ states.
243â†’     *
244â†’     * **A child must store the resulting [ChildHandle] and [dispose][DisposableHandle.dispose] the attachment
245â†’     * to its parent on its own completion.**
246â†’     *
247â†’     * Coroutine builders and job factory functions that accept `parent` [CoroutineContext] parameter
248â†’     * lookup a [Job] instance in the parent context and use this function to attach themselves as a child.
249â†’     * They also store a reference to the resulting [ChildHandle] and dispose a handle when they complete.
250â†’     *
251â†’     * @suppress This is an internal API. This method is too error prone for public API.
252â†’     */
253â†’    // ChildJob and ChildHandle are made internal on purpose to further deter 3rd-party impl of Job
254â†’    @InternalCoroutinesApi
255â†’    public fun attachChild(child: ChildJob): ChildHandle
256â†’
257â†’    // ------------ state waiting ------------
258â†’
259â†’    /**
260â†’     * Suspends the coroutine until this job is complete. This invocation resumes normally (without exception)
261â†’     * when the job is complete for any reason and the [Job] of the invoking coroutine is still [active][isActive].
262â†’     * This function also [starts][Job.start] the corresponding coroutine if the [Job] was still in _new_ state.
263â†’     *
264â†’     * Note that the job becomes complete only when all its children are complete.
265â†’     *
266â†’     * This suspending function is cancellable and **always** checks for a cancellation of the invoking coroutine's Job.
267â†’     * If the [Job] of the invoking coroutine is cancelled or completed when this
268â†’     * suspending function is invoked or while it is suspended, this function
269â†’     * throws [CancellationException].
270â†’     *
271â†’     * In particular, it means that a parent coroutine invoking `join` on a child coroutine throws
272â†’     * [CancellationException] if the child had failed, since a failure of a child coroutine cancels parent by default,
273â†’     * unless the child was launched from within [supervisorScope].
274â†’     *
275â†’     * This function can be used in [select] invocation with [onJoin] clause.
276â†’     * Use [isCompleted] to check for a completion of this job without waiting.
277â†’     *
278â†’     * There is [cancelAndJoin] function that combines an invocation of [cancel] and `join`.
279â†’     */
280â†’    public suspend fun join()
281â†’
282â†’    /**
283â†’     * Clause for [select] expression of [join] suspending function that selects when the job is complete.
284â†’     * This clause never fails, even if the job completes exceptionally.
285â†’     */
286â†’    public val onJoin: SelectClause0
287â†’
288â†’    // ------------ low-level state-notification ------------
289â†’
290â†’    /**
291â†’     * Registers handler that is **synchronously** invoked once on completion of this job.
292â†’     * When the job is already complete, then the handler is immediately invoked
293â†’     * with the job's exception or cancellation cause or `null`. Otherwise, the handler will be invoked once when this
294â†’     * job is complete.
295â†’     *
296â†’     * The meaning of `cause` that is passed to the handler:
297â†’     * * Cause is `null` when the job has completed normally.
298â†’     * * Cause is an instance of [CancellationException] when the job was cancelled _normally_.
299â†’     *   **It should not be treated as an error**. In particular, it should not be reported to error logs.
300â†’     * * Otherwise, the job had _failed_.
301â†’     *
302â†’     * The resulting [DisposableHandle] can be used to [dispose][DisposableHandle.dispose] the
303â†’     * registration of this handler and release its memory if its invocation is no longer needed.
304â†’     * There is no need to dispose the handler after completion of this job. The references to
305â†’     * all the handlers are released when this job completes.
306â†’     *
307â†’     * Installed [handler] should not throw any exceptions. If it does, they will get caught,
308â†’     * wrapped into [CompletionHandlerException], and rethrown, potentially causing crash of unrelated code.
309â†’     *
310â†’     * **Note**: Implementation of `CompletionHandler` must be fast, non-blocking, and thread-safe.
311â†’     * This handler can be invoked concurrently with the surrounding code.
312â†’     * There is no guarantee on the execution context in which the [handler] is invoked.
313â†’     */
314â†’    public fun invokeOnCompletion(handler: CompletionHandler): DisposableHandle
315â†’
316â†’    /**
317â†’     * Registers handler that is **synchronously** invoked once on cancellation or completion of this job.
318â†’     * when the job was already cancelled and is completed its execution, then the handler is immediately invoked
319â†’     * with the job's cancellation cause or `null` unless [invokeImmediately] is set to false.
320â†’     * Otherwise, handler will be invoked once when this job is cancelled or is complete.
321â†’     *
322â†’     * The meaning of `cause` that is passed to the handler:
323â†’     * * Cause is `null` when the job has completed normally.
324â†’     * * Cause is an instance of [CancellationException] when the job was cancelled _normally_.
325â†’     *   **It should not be treated as an error**. In particular, it should not be reported to error logs.
326â†’     * * Otherwise, the job had _failed_.
327â†’     *
328â†’     * Invocation of this handler on a transition to a _cancelling_ state
329â†’     * is controlled by [onCancelling] boolean parameter.
330â†’     * The handler is invoked when the job becomes _cancelling_ if [onCancelling] parameter is set to `true`.
331â†’     *
332â†’     * The resulting [DisposableHandle] can be used to [dispose][DisposableHandle.dispose] the
333â†’     * registration of this handler and release its memory if its invocation is no longer needed.
334â†’     * There is no need to dispose the handler after completion of this job. The references to
335â†’     * all the handlers are released when this job completes.
336â†’     *
337â†’     * Installed [handler] should not throw any exceptions. If it does, they will get caught,
338â†’     * wrapped into [CompletionHandlerException], and rethrown, potentially causing crash of unrelated code.
339â†’     *
340â†’     * **Note**: This function is a part of internal machinery that supports parent-child hierarchies
341â†’     * and allows for implementation of suspending functions that wait on the Job's state.
342â†’     * This function should not be used in general application code.
343â†’     * Implementation of `CompletionHandler` must be fast, non-blocking, and thread-safe.
344â†’     * This handler can be invoked concurrently with the surrounding code.
345â†’     * There is no guarantee on the execution context in which the [handler] is invoked.
346â†’     *
347â†’     * @param onCancelling when `true`, then the [handler] is invoked as soon as this job transitions to _cancelling_ state;
348â†’     *        when `false` then the [handler] is invoked only when it transitions to _completed_ state.
349â†’     * @param invokeImmediately when `true` and this job is already in the desired state (depending on [onCancelling]),
350â†’     *        then the [handler] is immediately and synchronously invoked and no-op [DisposableHandle] is returned;
351â†’     *        when `false` then no-op [DisposableHandle] is returned, but the [handler] is not invoked.
352â†’     * @param handler the handler.
353â†’     *
354â†’     * @suppress **This an internal API and should not be used from general code.**
355â†’     */
356â†’    @InternalCoroutinesApi
357â†’    public fun invokeOnCompletion(
358â†’        onCancelling: Boolean = false,
359â†’        invokeImmediately: Boolean = true,
360â†’        handler: CompletionHandler): DisposableHandle
361â†’
362â†’    // ------------ unstable internal API ------------
363â†’
364â†’    /**
365â†’     * @suppress **Error**: Operator '+' on two Job objects is meaningless.
366â†’     * Job is a coroutine context element and `+` is a set-sum operator for coroutine contexts.
367â†’     * The job to the right of `+` just replaces the job the left of `+`.
368â†’     */
369â†’    @Suppress("DeprecatedCallableAddReplaceWith")
370â†’    @Deprecated(message = "Operator '+' on two Job objects is meaningless. " +
371â†’        "Job is a coroutine context element and `+` is a set-sum operator for coroutine contexts. " +
372â†’        "The job to the right of `+` just replaces the job the left of `+`.",
373â†’        level = DeprecationLevel.ERROR)
374â†’    public operator fun plus(other: Job): Job = other
375â†’}
376â†’
377â†’/**
378â†’ * Creates a job object in an active state.
379â†’ * A failure of any child of this job immediately causes this job to fail, too, and cancels the rest of its children.
380â†’ *
381â†’ * To handle children failure independently of each other use [SupervisorJob].
382â†’ *
383â†’ * If [parent] job is specified, then this job becomes a child job of its parent and
384â†’ * is cancelled when its parent fails or is cancelled. All this job's children are cancelled in this case, too.
385â†’ *
386â†’ * Conceptually, the resulting job works in the same way as the job created by the `launch { body }` invocation
387â†’ * (see [launch]), but without any code in the body. It is active until cancelled or completed. Invocation of
388â†’ * [CompletableJob.complete] or [CompletableJob.completeExceptionally] corresponds to the successful or
389â†’ * failed completion of the body of the coroutine.
390â†’ *
391â†’ * @param parent an optional parent job.
392â†’ */
393â†’@Suppress("FunctionName")
394â†’public fun Job(parent: Job? = null): CompletableJob = JobImpl(parent)
395â†’
396â†’/** @suppress Binary compatibility only */
397â†’@Suppress("FunctionName")
398â†’@Deprecated(level = DeprecationLevel.HIDDEN, message = "Since 1.2.0, binary compatibility with versions <= 1.1.x")
399â†’@JvmName("Job")
400â†’public fun Job0(parent: Job? = null): Job = Job(parent)
401â†’
402â†’/**
403â†’ * A handle to an allocated object that can be disposed to make it eligible for garbage collection.
404â†’ */
405â†’public fun interface DisposableHandle {
406â†’    /**
407â†’     * Disposes the corresponding object, making it eligible for garbage collection.
408â†’     * Repeated invocation of this function has no effect.
409â†’     */
410â†’    public fun dispose()
411â†’}
412â†’
413â†’// -------------------- Parent-child communication --------------------
414â†’
415â†’/**
416â†’ * A reference that parent receives from its child so that it can report its cancellation.
417â†’ *
418â†’ * @suppress **This is unstable API and it is subject to change.**
419â†’ */
420â†’@InternalCoroutinesApi
421â†’@Deprecated(level = DeprecationLevel.ERROR, message = "This is internal API and may be removed in the future releases")
422â†’public interface ChildJob : Job {
423â†’    /**
424â†’     * Parent is cancelling its child by invoking this method.
425â†’     * Child finds the cancellation cause using [ParentJob.getChildJobCancellationCause].
426â†’     * This method does nothing is the child is already being cancelled.
427â†’     *
428â†’     * @suppress **This is unstable API and it is subject to change.**
429â†’     */
430â†’    @InternalCoroutinesApi
431â†’    public fun parentCancelled(parentJob: ParentJob)
432â†’}
433â†’
434â†’/**
435â†’ * A reference that child receives from its parent when it is being cancelled by the parent.
436â†’ *
437â†’ * @suppress **This is unstable API and it is subject to change.**
438â†’ */
439â†’@InternalCoroutinesApi
440â†’@Deprecated(level = DeprecationLevel.ERROR, message = "This is internal API and may be removed in the future releases")
441â†’public interface ParentJob : Job {
442â†’    /**
443â†’     * Child job is using this method to learn its cancellation cause when the parent cancels it with [ChildJob.parentCancelled].
444â†’     * This method is invoked only if the child was not already being cancelled.
445â†’     *
446â†’     * Note that [CancellationException] is the method's return type: if child is cancelled by its parent,
447â†’     * then the original exception is **already** handled by either the parent or the original source of failure.
448â†’     *
449â†’     * @suppress **This is unstable API and it is subject to change.**
450â†’     */
451â†’    @InternalCoroutinesApi
452â†’    public fun getChildJobCancellationCause(): CancellationException
453â†’}
454â†’
455â†’/**
456â†’ * A handle that child keep onto its parent so that it is able to report its cancellation.
457â†’ *
458â†’ * @suppress **This is unstable API and it is subject to change.**
459â†’ */
460â†’@InternalCoroutinesApi
461â†’@Deprecated(level = DeprecationLevel.ERROR, message = "This is internal API and may be removed in the future releases")
462â†’public interface ChildHandle : DisposableHandle {
463â†’
464â†’    /**
465â†’     * Returns the parent of the current parent-child relationship.
466â†’     * @suppress **This is unstable API and it is subject to change.**
467â†’     */
468â†’    @InternalCoroutinesApi
469â†’    public val parent: Job?
470â†’
471â†’    /**
472â†’     * Child is cancelling its parent by invoking this method.
473â†’     * This method is invoked by the child twice. The first time child report its root cause as soon as possible,
474â†’     * so that all its siblings and the parent can start cancelling their work asap. The second time
475â†’     * child invokes this method when it had aggregated and determined its final cancellation cause.
476â†’     *
477â†’     * @suppress **This is unstable API and it is subject to change.**
478â†’     */
479â†’    @InternalCoroutinesApi
480â†’    public fun childCancelled(cause: Throwable): Boolean
481â†’}
482â†’
483â†’// -------------------- Job extensions --------------------
484â†’
485â†’/**
486â†’ * Disposes a specified [handle] when this job is complete.
487â†’ *
488â†’ * This is a shortcut for the following code with slightly more efficient implementation (one fewer object created).
489â†’ * ```
490â†’ * invokeOnCompletion { handle.dispose() }
491â†’ * ```
492â†’ */
493â†’internal fun Job.disposeOnCompletion(handle: DisposableHandle): DisposableHandle =
494â†’    invokeOnCompletion(handler = DisposeOnCompletion(handle).asHandler)
495â†’
496â†’/**
497â†’ * Cancels the job and suspends the invoking coroutine until the cancelled job is complete.
498â†’ *
499â†’ * This suspending function is cancellable and **always** checks for a cancellation of the invoking coroutine's Job.
500â†’ * If the [Job] of the invoking coroutine is cancelled or completed when this
501â†’ * suspending function is invoked or while it is suspended, this function
502â†’ * throws [CancellationException].
503â†’ *
504â†’ * In particular, it means that a parent coroutine invoking `cancelAndJoin` on a child coroutine throws
505â†’ * [CancellationException] if the child had failed, since a failure of a child coroutine cancels parent by default,
506â†’ * unless the child was launched from within [supervisorScope].
507â†’ *
508â†’ * This is a shortcut for the invocation of [cancel][Job.cancel] followed by [join][Job.join].
509â†’ */
510â†’public suspend fun Job.cancelAndJoin() {
511â†’    cancel()
512â†’    return join()
513â†’}
514â†’
515â†’/**
516â†’ * Cancels all [children][Job.children] jobs of this coroutine using [Job.cancel] for all of them
517â†’ * with an optional cancellation [cause].
518â†’ * Unlike [Job.cancel] on this job as a whole, the state of this job itself is not affected.
519â†’ */
520â†’public fun Job.cancelChildren(cause: CancellationException? = null) {
521â†’    children.forEach { it.cancel(cause) }
522â†’}
523â†’
524â†’/**
525â†’ * @suppress This method implements old version of JVM ABI. Use [cancel].
526â†’ */
527â†’@Deprecated(level = DeprecationLevel.HIDDEN, message = "Since 1.2.0, binary compatibility with versions <= 1.1.x")
528â†’public fun Job.cancelChildren(): Unit = cancelChildren(null)
529â†’
530â†’/**
531â†’ * @suppress This method has bad semantics when cause is not a [CancellationException]. Use [Job.cancelChildren].
532â†’ */
533â†’@Deprecated(level = DeprecationLevel.HIDDEN, message = "Since 1.2.0, binary compatibility with versions <= 1.1.x")
534â†’public fun Job.cancelChildren(cause: Throwable? = null) {
535â†’    children.forEach { (it as? JobSupport)?.cancelInternal(cause.orCancellation(this)) }
536â†’}
537â†’
538â†’// -------------------- CoroutineContext extensions --------------------
539â†’
540â†’/**
541â†’ * Returns `true` when the [Job] of the coroutine in this context is still active
542â†’ * (has not completed and was not cancelled yet).

545â†’ * when [CoroutineScope.isActive] is not available:
546â†’ *
547â†’ * ```
548â†’ * while (coroutineContext.isActive) {
549â†’ *     // do some computation
550â†’ * }
551â†’ * ```

554â†’ * See [Job.isActive].

556â†’public val CoroutineContext.isActive: Boolean
557â†’    get() = this[Job]?.isActive == true
558â†’
559â†’/**
560â†’ * Cancels [Job] of this context with an optional cancellation cause.

562â†’ */
563â†’public fun CoroutineContext.cancel(cause: CancellationException? = null) {
564â†’    this[Job]?.cancel(cause)
565â†’}
566â†’
567â†’/**
568â†’ * @suppress This method implements old version of JVM ABI. Use [CoroutineContext.cancel].
569â†’ */
570â†’@Deprecated(level = DeprecationLevel.HIDDEN, message = "Since 1.2.0, binary compatibility with versions <= 1.1.x")
571â†’public fun CoroutineContext.cancel(): Unit = cancel(null)
572â†’
573â†’/**
574â†’ * Ensures that current job is [active][Job.isActive].
575â†’ * If the job is no longer active, throws [CancellationException].
576â†’ * If the job was cancelled, thrown exception contains the original cancellation cause.
577â†’ *
578â†’ * This method is a drop-in replacement for the following code, but with more precise exception:
579â†’ * ```
580â†’ * if (!job.isActive) {
581â†’ *     throw CancellationException()
582â†’ * }
583â†’ * ```
584â†’ */
585â†’public fun Job.ensureActive(): Unit {
586â†’    if (!isActive) throw getCancellationException()
587â†’}
588â†’
589â†’/**
590â†’ * Ensures that job in the current context is [active][Job.isActive].
591â†’ *
592â†’ * If the job is no longer active, throws [CancellationException].
593â†’ * If the job was cancelled, thrown exception contains the original cancellation cause.
594â†’ * This function does not do anything if there is no [Job] in the context, since such a coroutine cannot be cancelled.
595â†’ *
596â†’ * This method is a drop-in replacement for the following code, but with more precise exception:
597â†’ * ```
598â†’ * if (!isActive) {
599â†’ *     throw CancellationException()
600â†’ * }
601â†’ * ```
602â†’ */
603â†’public fun CoroutineContext.ensureActive() {
604â†’    get(Job)?.ensureActive()
605â†’}
606â†’
607â†’/**
608â†’ * Cancels current job, including all its children with a specified diagnostic error [message].
609â†’ * A [cause] can be specified to provide additional details on a cancellation reason for debugging purposes.
610â†’ */
611â†’public fun Job.cancel(message: String, cause: Throwable? = null): Unit = cancel(CancellationException(message, cause))
612â†’
613â†’/**
614â†’ * @suppress This method has bad semantics when cause is not a [CancellationException]. Use [CoroutineContext.cancel].
615â†’ */
616â†’@Deprecated(level = DeprecationLevel.HIDDEN, message = "Since 1.2.0, binary compatibility with versions <= 1.1.x")
617â†’public fun CoroutineContext.cancel(cause: Throwable? = null): Boolean {
618â†’    val job = this[Job] as? JobSupport ?: return false
619â†’    job.cancelInternal(cause.orCancellation(job))

621â†’}
622â†’
623â†’/**
624â†’ * Cancels all children of the [Job] in this context, without touching the state of this job itself
625â†’ * with an optional cancellation cause. See [Job.cancel].
626â†’ * It does not do anything if there is no job in the context or it has no children.
627â†’ */
628â†’public fun CoroutineContext.cancelChildren(cause: CancellationException? = null) {
629â†’    this[Job]?.children?.forEach { it.cancel(cause) }
630â†’}
631â†’
632â†’/**
633â†’ * @suppress This method implements old version of JVM ABI. Use [CoroutineContext.cancelChildren].
634â†’ */
635â†’@Deprecated(level = DeprecationLevel.HIDDEN, message = "Since 1.2.0, binary compatibility with versions <= 1.1.x")
636â†’public fun CoroutineContext.cancelChildren(): Unit = cancelChildren(null)
637â†’
638â†’/**
639â†’ * Retrieves the current [Job] instance from the given [CoroutineContext] or
640â†’ * throws [IllegalStateException] if no job is present in the context.
641â†’ *
642â†’ * This method is a short-cut for `coroutineContext[Job]!!` and should be used only when it is known in advance that
643â†’ * the context does have instance of the job in it.
644â†’ */
645â†’public val CoroutineContext.job: Job get() = get(Job) ?: error("Current context doesn't contain Job in it: $this")
646â†’
647â†’/**
648â†’ * @suppress This method has bad semantics when cause is not a [CancellationException]. Use [CoroutineContext.cancelChildren].
649â†’ */
650â†’@Deprecated(level = DeprecationLevel.HIDDEN, message = "Since 1.2.0, binary compatibility with versions <= 1.1.x")
651â†’public fun CoroutineContext.cancelChildren(cause: Throwable? = null) {
652â†’    val job = this[Job] ?: return
653â†’    job.children.forEach { (it as? JobSupport)?.cancelInternal(cause.orCancellation(job)) }

655â†’
656â†’private fun Throwable?.orCancellation(job: Job): Throwable = this ?: JobCancellationException("Job was cancelled", null, job)
657â†’
658â†’/**
659â†’ * No-op implementation of [DisposableHandle].
660â†’ * @suppress **This an internal API and should not be used from general code.**
661â†’ */

663â†’public object NonDisposableHandle : DisposableHandle, ChildHandle {

665â†’    override val parent: Job? get() = null
666â†’
667â†’    /**
668â†’     * Does not do anything.
669â†’     * @suppress
670â†’     */
671â†’    override fun dispose() {}
672â†’
673â†’    /**
674â†’     * Returns `false`.
675â†’     * @suppress
676â†’     */
677â†’    override fun childCancelled(cause: Throwable): Boolean = false
678â†’
679â†’    /**
680â†’     * Returns "NonDisposableHandle" string.
681â†’     * @suppress
682â†’     */

@file:JvmMultifileClass
@file:JvmName("JobKt")
@file:Suppress("DEPRECATION_ERROR", "RedundantUnitReturnType")

package kotlinx.coroutines

import kotlinx.coroutines.selects.*
import kotlin.coroutines.*
import kotlin.jvm.*

// --------------- core job interfaces ---------------

/**
 * A background job. Conceptually, a job is a cancellable thing with a life-cycle that
 * culminates in its completion.
 *
 * Jobs can be arranged into parent-child hierarchies where cancellation
 * of a parent leads to immediate cancellation of all its [children] recursively.
 * Failure of a child with an exception other than [CancellationException] immediately cancels its parent and,
 * consequently, all its other children. This behavior can be customized using [SupervisorJob].
 *
 * The most basic instances of `Job` interface are created like this:
 *
 * * **Coroutine job** is created with [launch][CoroutineScope.launch] coroutine builder.
 *   It runs a specified block of code and completes on completion of this block.
 * * **[CompletableJob]** is created with a `Job()` factory function.
 *   It is completed by calling [CompletableJob.complete].
 *
 * Conceptually, an execution of a job does not produce a result value. Jobs are launched solely for their
 * side effects. See [Deferred] interface for a job that produces a result.
 *
 * ### Job states
 *
 * A job has the following states:
 *
 * | **State**                        | [isActive] | [isCompleted] | [isCancelled] |
 * | -------------------------------- | ---------- | ------------- | ------------- |
 * | _New_ (optional initial state)   | `false`    | `false`       | `false`       |
 * | _Active_ (default initial state) | `true`     | `false`       | `false`       |
 * | _Completing_ (transient state)   | `true`     | `false`       | `false`       |
 * | _Cancelling_ (transient state)   | `false`    | `false`       | `true`        |
 * | _Cancelled_ (final state)        | `false`    | `true`        | `true`        |
 * | _Completed_ (final state)        | `false`    | `true`        | `false`       |
 *
 * Usually, a job is created in the _active_ state (it is created and started). However, coroutine builders
 * that provide an optional `start` parameter create a coroutine in the _new_ state when this parameter is set to
 * [CoroutineStart.LAZY]. Such a job can be made _active_ by invoking [start] or [join].
 *
 * A job is _active_ while the coroutine is working or until [CompletableJob] is completed,
 * or until it fails or cancelled.
 *
 * Failure of an _active_ job with an exception makes it _cancelling_.
 * A job can be cancelled at any time with [cancel] function that forces it to transition to
 * the _cancelling_ state immediately. The job becomes _cancelled_  when it finishes executing its work and
 * all its children complete.
 *
 * Completion of an _active_ coroutine's body or a call to [CompletableJob.complete] transitions the job to
 * the _completing_ state. It waits in the _completing_ state for all its children to complete before
 * transitioning to the _completed_ state.
 * Note that _completing_ state is purely internal to the job. For an outside observer a _completing_ job is still
 * active, while internally it is waiting for its children.
 *
 * ```
 *                                       wait children
 * +-----+ start  +--------+ complete   +-------------+  finish  +-----------+
 * | New | -----> | Active | ---------> | Completing  | -------> | Completed |
 * +-----+        +--------+            +-------------+          +-----------+
 *                  |  cancel / fail       |
 *                  |     +----------------+
 *                  |     |
 *                  V     V
 *              +------------+                           finish  +-----------+
 *              | Cancelling | --------------------------------> | Cancelled |
 *              +------------+                                   +-----------+
 * ```
 *
 * A `Job` instance in the
 * [coroutineContext](https://kotlinlang.org/api/latest/jvm/stdlib/kotlin.coroutines/coroutine-context.html)
 * represents the coroutine itself.
 *
 * ### Cancellation cause
 *
 * A coroutine job is said to _complete exceptionally_ when its body throws an exception;
 * a [CompletableJob] is completed exceptionally by calling [CompletableJob.completeExceptionally].
 * An exceptionally completed job is cancelled and the corresponding exception becomes the _cancellation cause_ of the job.
 *
 * Normal cancellation of a job is distinguished from its failure by the type of this exception that caused its cancellation.
 * A coroutine that threw [CancellationException] is considered to be _cancelled normally_.
 * If a cancellation cause is a different exception type, then the job is considered to have _failed_.
 * When a job has _failed_, then its parent gets cancelled with the exception of the same type,
 * thus ensuring transparency in delegating parts of the job to its children.
 *
 * Note, that [cancel] function on a job only accepts [CancellationException] as a cancellation cause, thus
 * calling [cancel] always results in a normal cancellation of a job, which does not lead to cancellation
 * of its parent. This way, a parent can [cancel] its own children (cancelling all their children recursively, too)
 * without cancelling itself.
 *
 * ### Concurrency and synchronization
 *
 * All functions on this interface and on all interfaces derived from it are **thread-safe** and can
 * be safely invoked from concurrent coroutines without external synchronization.
 *
 * ### Not stable for inheritance
 *
 * **`Job` interface and all its derived interfaces are not stable for inheritance in 3rd party libraries**,

     * all the handlers are released when this job completes.

 * Creates a job object in an active state.

@Deprecated(level = DeprecationLevel.HIDDEN, message = "Since 1.2.0, binary compatibility with versions <= 1.1.x")
@JvmName("Job")

/**
 * A handle to an allocated object that can be disposed to make it eligible for garbage collection.
 */
public fun interface DisposableHandle {

     * Disposes the corresponding object, making it eligible for garbage collection.
     * Repeated invocation of this function has no effect.
     */

/**
 * A reference that parent receives from its child so that it can report its cancellation.
 *
 * @suppress **This is unstable API and it is subject to change.**
 */
@InternalCoroutinesApi
@Deprecated(level = DeprecationLevel.ERROR, message = "This is internal API and may be removed in the future releases")
public interface ChildJob : Job {

/**

 * @suppress **This is unstable API and it is subject to change.**

@InternalCoroutinesApi
@Deprecated(level = DeprecationLevel.ERROR, message = "This is internal API and may be removed in the future releases")
public interface ParentJob : Job {

@InternalCoroutinesApi
@Deprecated(level = DeprecationLevel.ERROR, message = "This is internal API and may be removed in the future releases")

public fun CoroutineContext.cancelChildren(): Unit = cancelChildren(null)

@Deprecated(level = DeprecationLevel.HIDDEN, message = "Since 1.2.0, binary compatibility with versions <= 1.1.x")
public fun CoroutineContext.cancelChildren(cause: Throwable? = null) {

private fun Throwable?.orCancellation(job: Job): Throwable = this ?: JobCancellationException("Job was cancelled", null, job)

@InternalCoroutinesApi
public object NonDisposableHandle : DisposableHandle, ChildHandle {

     * @suppress

    override fun toString(): String = "NonDisposableHandle"

1â†’<!--- TEST_NAME DispatcherGuideTest -->
  2â†’
  3â†’[//]: # (title: Coroutine context and dispatchers)
  4â†’
  5â†’Coroutines always execute in some context represented by a value of the 
  6â†’[CoroutineContext](https://kotlinlang.org/api/latest/jvm/stdlib/kotlin.coroutines/-coroutine-context/) 
  7â†’type, defined in the Kotlin standard library.
  8â†’
  9â†’The coroutine context is a set of various elements. The main elements are the [Job] of the coroutine, 
 10â†’which we've seen before, and its dispatcher, which is covered in this section.
 11â†’
 12â†’## Dispatchers and threads
 13â†’
 14â†’The coroutine context includes a _coroutine dispatcher_ (see [CoroutineDispatcher]) that determines what thread or threads 
 15â†’the corresponding coroutine uses for its execution. The coroutine dispatcher can confine coroutine execution 
 16â†’to a specific thread, dispatch it to a thread pool, or let it run unconfined. 
 17â†’
 18â†’All coroutine builders like [launch] and [async] accept an optional 
 19â†’[CoroutineContext](https://kotlinlang.org/api/latest/jvm/stdlib/kotlin.coroutines/-coroutine-context/) 
 20â†’parameter that can be used to explicitly specify the dispatcher for the new coroutine and other context elements. 
 21â†’
 22â†’Try the following example:
 23â†’
 24â†’```kotlin
 25â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
 26â†’
 27â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
 28â†’//sampleStart
 29â†’    launch { // context of the parent, main runBlocking coroutine
 30â†’        println("main runBlocking      : I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
 31â†’    }
 32â†’    launch(Dispatchers.Unconfined) { // not confined -- will work with main thread
 33â†’        println("Unconfined            : I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
 34â†’    }
 35â†’    launch(Dispatchers.Default) { // will get dispatched to DefaultDispatcher 
 36â†’        println("Default               : I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
 37â†’    }
 38â†’    launch(newSingleThreadContext("MyOwnThread")) { // will get its own new thread
 39â†’        println("newSingleThreadContext: I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
 40â†’    }
 41â†’//sampleEnd    
 42â†’}
 43â†’```
 44â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
 45â†’
 46â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-01.kt).
 47â†’>
 48â†’{type="note"}
 49â†’
 50â†’It produces the following output (maybe in different order):
 51â†’
 52â†’```text
 53â†’Unconfined            : I'm working in thread main
 54â†’Default               : I'm working in thread DefaultDispatcher-worker-1
 55â†’newSingleThreadContext: I'm working in thread MyOwnThread
 56â†’main runBlocking      : I'm working in thread main
 57â†’```
 58â†’
 59â†’<!--- TEST LINES_START_UNORDERED -->
 60â†’
 61â†’When `launch { ... }` is used without parameters, it inherits the context (and thus dispatcher)
 62â†’from the [CoroutineScope] it is being launched from. In this case, it inherits the
 63â†’context of the main `runBlocking` coroutine which runs in the `main` thread. 
 64â†’
 65â†’[Dispatchers.Unconfined] is a special dispatcher that also appears to run in the `main` thread, but it is,
 66â†’in fact, a different mechanism that is explained later.
 67â†’
 68â†’The default dispatcher is used when no other dispatcher is explicitly specified in the scope.
 69â†’It is represented by [Dispatchers.Default] and uses a shared background pool of threads.
 70â†’  
 71â†’[newSingleThreadContext] creates a thread for the coroutine to run. 
 72â†’A dedicated thread is a very expensive resource. 
 73â†’In a real application it must be either released, when no longer needed, using the [close][ExecutorCoroutineDispatcher.close] 
 74â†’function, or stored in a top-level variable and reused throughout the application.  
 75â†’
 76â†’## Unconfined vs confined dispatcher
 77â†’ 
 78â†’The [Dispatchers.Unconfined] coroutine dispatcher starts a coroutine in the caller thread, but only until the
 79â†’first suspension point. After suspension it resumes the coroutine in the thread that is fully determined by the
 80â†’suspending function that was invoked. The unconfined dispatcher is appropriate for coroutines which neither
 81â†’consume CPU time nor update any shared data (like UI) confined to a specific thread. 
 82â†’
 83â†’On the other side, the dispatcher is inherited from the outer [CoroutineScope] by default. 
 84â†’The default dispatcher for the [runBlocking] coroutine, in particular,
 85â†’is confined to the invoker thread, so inheriting it has the effect of confining execution to
 86â†’this thread with predictable FIFO scheduling.
 87â†’
 88â†’```kotlin
 89â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
 90â†’
 91â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
 92â†’//sampleStart
 93â†’    launch(Dispatchers.Unconfined) { // not confined -- will work with main thread
 94â†’        println("Unconfined      : I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
 95â†’        delay(500)
 96â†’        println("Unconfined      : After delay in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
 97â†’    }
 98â†’    launch { // context of the parent, main runBlocking coroutine
 99â†’        println("main runBlocking: I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
100â†’        delay(1000)
101â†’        println("main runBlocking: After delay in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
102â†’    }
103â†’//sampleEnd    
104â†’}
105â†’```
106â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
107â†’
108â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-02.kt).
109â†’>
110â†’{type="note"}
111â†’
112â†’Produces the output: 
113â†’ 
114â†’```text
115â†’Unconfined      : I'm working in thread main
116â†’main runBlocking: I'm working in thread main
117â†’Unconfined      : After delay in thread kotlinx.coroutines.DefaultExecutor
118â†’main runBlocking: After delay in thread main
119â†’```
120â†’
121â†’<!--- TEST LINES_START -->
122â†’ 
123â†’So, the coroutine with the context inherited from `runBlocking {...}` continues to execute
124â†’in the `main` thread, while the unconfined one resumes in the default executor thread that the [delay]
125â†’function is using.
126â†’
127â†’> The unconfined dispatcher is an advanced mechanism that can be helpful in certain corner cases where
128â†’> dispatching of a coroutine for its execution later is not needed or produces undesirable side-effects,
129â†’> because some operation in a coroutine must be performed right away. 
130â†’> The unconfined dispatcher should not be used in general code. 
131â†’>
132â†’{type="note"}
133â†’
134â†’## Debugging coroutines and threads
135â†’
136â†’Coroutines can suspend on one thread and resume on another thread. 
137â†’Even with a single-threaded dispatcher it might be hard to
138â†’figure out what the coroutine was doing, where, and when if you don't have special tooling. 
139â†’
140â†’### Debugging with IDEA
141â†’
142â†’The Coroutine Debugger of the Kotlin plugin simplifies debugging coroutines in IntelliJ IDEA.
143â†’
144â†’> Debugging works for versions 1.3.8 or later of `kotlinx-coroutines-core`.
145â†’>
146â†’{type="note"}
147â†’
148â†’The **Debug** tool window contains the **Coroutines** tab. In this tab, you can find information about both currently running and suspended coroutines. 
149â†’The coroutines are grouped by the dispatcher they are running on.
150â†’
151â†’![Debugging coroutines](coroutine-idea-debugging-1.png){width=700}
152â†’
153â†’With the coroutine debugger, you can:
154â†’* Check the state of each coroutine.
155â†’* See the values of local and captured variables for both running and suspended coroutines.
156â†’* See a full coroutine creation stack, as well as a call stack inside the coroutine. The stack includes all frames with 
157â†’variable values, even those that would be lost during standard debugging.
158â†’* Get a full report that contains the state of each coroutine and its stack. To obtain it, right-click inside the **Coroutines** tab, and then click **Get Coroutines Dump**.
159â†’
160â†’To start coroutine debugging, you just need to set breakpoints and run the application in debug mode.
161â†’
162â†’Learn more about coroutines debugging in the [tutorial](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/tutorials/coroutines/debug-coroutines-with-idea.html).
163â†’
164â†’### Debugging using logging
165â†’
166â†’Another approach to debugging applications with 
167â†’threads without Coroutine Debugger is to print the thread name in the log file on each log statement. This feature is universally supported
168â†’by logging frameworks. When using coroutines, the thread name alone does not give much of a context, so 
169â†’`kotlinx.coroutines` includes debugging facilities to make it easier. 
170â†’
171â†’Run the following code with `-Dkotlinx.coroutines.debug` JVM option:
172â†’
173â†’```kotlin
174â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
175â†’
176â†’fun log(msg: String) = println("[${Thread.currentThread().name}] $msg")
177â†’
178â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
179â†’//sampleStart
180â†’    val a = async {
181â†’        log("I'm computing a piece of the answer")
182â†’        6
183â†’    }
184â†’    val b = async {
185â†’        log("I'm computing another piece of the answer")
186â†’        7
187â†’    }
188â†’    log("The answer is ${a.await() * b.await()}")
189â†’//sampleEnd    
190â†’}
191â†’```
192â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
193â†’
194â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-03.kt).
195â†’>
196â†’{type="note"}
197â†’
198â†’There are three coroutines. The main coroutine (#1) inside `runBlocking` 
199â†’and two coroutines computing the deferred values `a` (#2) and `b` (#3).
200â†’They are all executing in the context of `runBlocking` and are confined to the main thread.
201â†’The output of this code is:
202â†’
203â†’```text
204â†’[main @coroutine#2] I'm computing a piece of the answer
205â†’[main @coroutine#3] I'm computing another piece of the answer
206â†’[main @coroutine#1] The answer is 42
207â†’```
208â†’
209â†’<!--- TEST FLEXIBLE_THREAD -->
210â†’
211â†’The `log` function prints the name of the thread in square brackets, and you can see that it is the `main`
212â†’thread with the identifier of the currently executing coroutine appended to it. This identifier 
213â†’is consecutively assigned to all created coroutines when the debugging mode is on.
214â†’
215â†’> Debugging mode is also turned on when JVM is run with `-ea` option.
216â†’> You can read more about debugging facilities in the documentation of the [DEBUG_PROPERTY_NAME] property.
217â†’>
218â†’{type="note"}
219â†’
220â†’## Jumping between threads
221â†’
222â†’Run the following code with the `-Dkotlinx.coroutines.debug` JVM option (see [debug](#debugging-coroutines-and-threads)):
223â†’
224â†’```kotlin
225â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
226â†’
227â†’fun log(msg: String) = println("[${Thread.currentThread().name}] $msg")
228â†’
229â†’fun main() {
230â†’//sampleStart
231â†’    newSingleThreadContext("Ctx1").use { ctx1 ->
232â†’        newSingleThreadContext("Ctx2").use { ctx2 ->
233â†’            runBlocking(ctx1) {
234â†’                log("Started in ctx1")
235â†’                withContext(ctx2) {
236â†’                    log("Working in ctx2")
237â†’                }
238â†’                log("Back to ctx1")
239â†’            }
240â†’        }
241â†’    }
242â†’//sampleEnd    
243â†’}
244â†’```
245â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
246â†’
247â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-04.kt).
248â†’>
249â†’{type="note"}
250â†’
251â†’It demonstrates several new techniques. One is using [runBlocking] with an explicitly specified context, and
252â†’the other one is using the [withContext] function to change the context of a coroutine while still staying in the
253â†’same coroutine, as you can see in the output below:
254â†’
255â†’```text
256â†’[Ctx1 @coroutine#1] Started in ctx1
257â†’[Ctx2 @coroutine#1] Working in ctx2
258â†’[Ctx1 @coroutine#1] Back to ctx1
259â†’```
260â†’
261â†’<!--- TEST -->
262â†’
263â†’Note that this example also uses the `use` function from the Kotlin standard library to release threads
264â†’created with [newSingleThreadContext] when they are no longer needed. 
265â†’
266â†’## Job in the context
267â†’
268â†’The coroutine's [Job] is part of its context, and can be retrieved from it 
269â†’using the `coroutineContext[Job]` expression:
270â†’
271â†’```kotlin
272â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
273â†’
274â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
275â†’//sampleStart
276â†’    println("My job is ${coroutineContext[Job]}")
277â†’//sampleEnd    
278â†’}
279â†’```
280â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
281â†’
282â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-05.kt).
283â†’> 
284â†’{type="note"}
285â†’
286â†’In the [debug mode](#debugging-coroutines-and-threads), it outputs something like this:
287â†’
288â†’```
289â†’My job is "coroutine#1":BlockingCoroutine{Active}@6d311334
290â†’```
291â†’
292â†’<!--- TEST lines.size == 1 && lines[0].startsWith("My job is \"coroutine#1\":BlockingCoroutine{Active}@") -->
293â†’
294â†’Note that [isActive] in [CoroutineScope] is just a convenient shortcut for
295â†’`coroutineContext[Job]?.isActive == true`.
296â†’
297â†’## Children of a coroutine
298â†’
299â†’When a coroutine is launched in the [CoroutineScope] of another coroutine,
300â†’it inherits its context via [CoroutineScope.coroutineContext] and 
301â†’the [Job] of the new coroutine becomes
302â†’a _child_ of the parent coroutine's job. When the parent coroutine is cancelled, all its children
303â†’are recursively cancelled, too. 
304â†’
305â†’However, this parent-child relation can be explicitly overriden in one of two ways:
306â†’
307â†’1. When a different scope is explicitly specified when launching a coroutine (for example, `GlobalScope.launch`), 
308â†’   then it does not inherit a `Job` from the parent scope.
309â†’2. When a different `Job` object is passed as the context for the new coroutine (as shown in the example below),
310â†’   then it overrides the `Job` of the parent scope.
311â†’   
312â†’In both cases, the launched coroutine is not tied to the scope it was launched from and operates independently.
313â†’
314â†’```kotlin
315â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
316â†’
317â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
318â†’//sampleStart
319â†’    // launch a coroutine to process some kind of incoming request
320â†’    val request = launch {
321â†’        // it spawns two other jobs
322â†’        launch(Job()) { 
323â†’            println("job1: I run in my own Job and execute independently!")
324â†’            delay(1000)
325â†’            println("job1: I am not affected by cancellation of the request")
326â†’        }
327â†’        // and the other inherits the parent context
328â†’        launch {
329â†’            delay(100)
330â†’            println("job2: I am a child of the request coroutine")
331â†’            delay(1000)
332â†’            println("job2: I will not execute this line if my parent request is cancelled")
333â†’        }
334â†’    }
335â†’    delay(500)
336â†’    request.cancel() // cancel processing of the request
337â†’    println("main: Who has survived request cancellation?")
338â†’    delay(1000) // delay the main thread for a second to see what happens
339â†’//sampleEnd
340â†’}
341â†’```
342â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
343â†’
344â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-06.kt).
345â†’>
346â†’{type="note"}
347â†’
348â†’The output of this code is:
349â†’
350â†’```text
351â†’job1: I run in my own Job and execute independently!
352â†’job2: I am a child of the request coroutine
353â†’main: Who has survived request cancellation?
354â†’job1: I am not affected by cancellation of the request
355â†’```
356â†’
357â†’<!--- TEST -->
358â†’
359â†’## Parental responsibilities 
360â†’
361â†’A parent coroutine always waits for completion of all its children. A parent does not have to explicitly track
362â†’all the children it launches, and it does not have to use [Job.join] to wait for them at the end:
363â†’
364â†’```kotlin
365â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
366â†’
367â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
368â†’//sampleStart
369â†’    // launch a coroutine to process some kind of incoming request
370â†’    val request = launch {
371â†’        repeat(3) { i -> // launch a few children jobs
372â†’            launch  {
373â†’                delay((i + 1) * 200L) // variable delay 200ms, 400ms, 600ms
374â†’                println("Coroutine $i is done")
375â†’            }
376â†’        }
377â†’        println("request: I'm done and I don't explicitly join my children that are still active")
378â†’    }
379â†’    request.join() // wait for completion of the request, including all its children
380â†’    println("Now processing of the request is complete")
381â†’//sampleEnd
382â†’}
383â†’```
384â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
385â†’
386â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-07.kt).
387â†’>
388â†’{type="note"}
389â†’
390â†’The result is going to be:
391â†’
392â†’```text
393â†’request: I'm done and I don't explicitly join my children that are still active
394â†’Coroutine 0 is done
395â†’Coroutine 1 is done
396â†’Coroutine 2 is done
397â†’Now processing of the request is complete
398â†’```
399â†’
400â†’<!--- TEST -->
401â†’
402â†’## Naming coroutines for debugging
403â†’
404â†’Automatically assigned ids are good when coroutines log often and you just need to correlate log records
405â†’coming from the same coroutine. However, when a coroutine is tied to the processing of a specific request
406â†’or doing some specific background task, it is better to name it explicitly for debugging purposes.
407â†’The [CoroutineName] context element serves the same purpose as the thread name. It is included in the thread name that
408â†’is executing this coroutine when the [debugging mode](#debugging-coroutines-and-threads) is turned on.
409â†’
410â†’The following example demonstrates this concept:
411â†’
412â†’```kotlin
413â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
414â†’
415â†’fun log(msg: String) = println("[${Thread.currentThread().name}] $msg")
416â†’
417â†’fun main() = runBlocking(CoroutineName("main")) {
418â†’//sampleStart
419â†’    log("Started main coroutine")
420â†’    // run two background value computations
421â†’    val v1 = async(CoroutineName("v1coroutine")) {
422â†’        delay(500)
423â†’        log("Computing v1")
424â†’        252
425â†’    }
426â†’    val v2 = async(CoroutineName("v2coroutine")) {
427â†’        delay(1000)
428â†’        log("Computing v2")
429â†’        6
430â†’    }
431â†’    log("The answer for v1 / v2 = ${v1.await() / v2.await()}")
432â†’//sampleEnd    
433â†’}
434â†’```
435â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
436â†’
437â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-08.kt).
438â†’>
439â†’{type="note"}
440â†’
441â†’The output it produces with `-Dkotlinx.coroutines.debug` JVM option is similar to:
442â†’ 
443â†’```text
444â†’[main @main#1] Started main coroutine
445â†’[main @v1coroutine#2] Computing v1
446â†’[main @v2coroutine#3] Computing v2
447â†’[main @main#1] The answer for v1 / v2 = 42
448â†’```
449â†’
450â†’<!--- TEST FLEXIBLE_THREAD -->
451â†’
452â†’## Combining context elements
453â†’
454â†’Sometimes we need to define multiple elements for a coroutine context. We can use the `+` operator for that.
455â†’For example, we can launch a coroutine with an explicitly specified dispatcher and an explicitly specified 
456â†’name at the same time: 
457â†’
458â†’```kotlin
459â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
460â†’
461â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
462â†’//sampleStart
463â†’    launch(Dispatchers.Default + CoroutineName("test")) {
464â†’        println("I'm working in thread ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
465â†’    }
466â†’//sampleEnd    
467â†’}
468â†’```
469â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
470â†’
471â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-09.kt).
472â†’>
473â†’{type="note"}
474â†’
475â†’The output of this code with the `-Dkotlinx.coroutines.debug` JVM option is: 
476â†’
477â†’```text
478â†’I'm working in thread DefaultDispatcher-worker-1 @test#2
479â†’```
480â†’
481â†’<!--- TEST FLEXIBLE_THREAD -->
482â†’
483â†’## Coroutine scope
484â†’
485â†’Let us put our knowledge about contexts, children and jobs together. Assume that our application has
486â†’an object with a lifecycle, but that object is not a coroutine. For example, we are writing an Android application
487â†’and launch various coroutines in the context of an Android activity to perform asynchronous operations to fetch 
488â†’and update data, do animations, etc. All of these coroutines must be cancelled when the activity is destroyed
489â†’to avoid memory leaks. We, of course, can manipulate contexts and jobs manually to tie the lifecycles of the activity 
490â†’and its coroutines, but `kotlinx.coroutines` provides an abstraction encapsulating that: [CoroutineScope].
491â†’You should be already familiar with the coroutine scope as all coroutine builders are declared as extensions on it. 
492â†’
493â†’We manage the lifecycles of our coroutines by creating an instance of [CoroutineScope] tied to 
494â†’the lifecycle of our activity. A `CoroutineScope` instance can be created by the [CoroutineScope()] or [MainScope()]
495â†’factory functions. The former creates a general-purpose scope, while the latter creates a scope for UI applications and uses
496â†’[Dispatchers.Main] as the default dispatcher:
497â†’
498â†’```kotlin
499â†’class Activity {
500â†’    private val mainScope = MainScope()
501â†’    
502â†’    fun destroy() {
503â†’        mainScope.cancel()
504â†’    }
505â†’    // to be continued ...
506â†’```
507â†’
508â†’Now, we can launch coroutines in the scope of this `Activity` using the defined `scope`.
509â†’For the demo, we launch ten coroutines that delay for a different time:
510â†’
511â†’```kotlin
512â†’    // class Activity continues
513â†’    fun doSomething() {
514â†’        // launch ten coroutines for a demo, each working for a different time
515â†’        repeat(10) { i ->
516â†’            mainScope.launch {
517â†’                delay((i + 1) * 200L) // variable delay 200ms, 400ms, ... etc
518â†’                println("Coroutine $i is done")
519â†’            }
520â†’        }
521â†’    }
522â†’} // class Activity ends
523â†’``` 
524â†’
525â†’In our main function we create the activity, call our test `doSomething` function, and destroy the activity after 500ms.
526â†’This cancels all the coroutines that were launched from `doSomething`. We can see that because after the destruction 
527â†’of the activity no more messages are printed, even if we wait a little longer.
528â†’
529â†’<!--- CLEAR -->
530â†’
531â†’```kotlin
532â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
533â†’
534â†’class Activity {
535â†’    private val mainScope = CoroutineScope(Dispatchers.Default) // use Default for test purposes
536â†’    
537â†’    fun destroy() {
538â†’        mainScope.cancel()
539â†’    }
540â†’
541â†’    fun doSomething() {
542â†’        // launch ten coroutines for a demo, each working for a different time
543â†’        repeat(10) { i ->
544â†’            mainScope.launch {
545â†’                delay((i + 1) * 200L) // variable delay 200ms, 400ms, ... etc
546â†’                println("Coroutine $i is done")
547â†’            }
548â†’        }
549â†’    }
550â†’} // class Activity ends
551â†’
552â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
553â†’//sampleStart
554â†’    val activity = Activity()
555â†’    activity.doSomething() // run test function
556â†’    println("Launched coroutines")
557â†’    delay(500L) // delay for half a second
558â†’    println("Destroying activity!")
559â†’    activity.destroy() // cancels all coroutines
560â†’    delay(1000) // visually confirm that they don't work
561â†’//sampleEnd    
562â†’}
563â†’```
564â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
565â†’
566â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-10.kt).
567â†’>
568â†’{type="note"}
569â†’
570â†’The output of this example is:
571â†’
572â†’```text
573â†’Launched coroutines
574â†’Coroutine 0 is done
575â†’Coroutine 1 is done
576â†’Destroying activity!
577â†’```
578â†’
579â†’<!--- TEST -->
580â†’
581â†’As you can see, only the first two coroutines print a message and the others are cancelled 
582â†’by a single invocation of `job.cancel()` in `Activity.destroy()`.
583â†’
584â†’> Note, that Android has first-party support for coroutine scope in all entities with the lifecycle.
585â†’> See [the corresponding documentation](https://developer.android.com/topic/libraries/architecture/coroutines#lifecyclescope).
586â†’>
587â†’{type="note"}
588â†’
589â†’### Thread-local data
590â†’
591â†’Sometimes it is convenient to have an ability to pass some thread-local data to or between coroutines. 
592â†’However, since they are not bound to any particular thread, this will likely lead to boilerplate if done manually.
593â†’
594â†’For [`ThreadLocal`](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/api/java/lang/ThreadLocal.html), 
595â†’the [asContextElement] extension function is here for the rescue. It creates an additional context element 
596â†’which keeps the value of the given `ThreadLocal` and restores it every time the coroutine switches its context.
597â†’
598â†’It is easy to demonstrate it in action:
599â†’
600â†’```kotlin
601â†’import kotlinx.coroutines.*
602â†’
603â†’val threadLocal = ThreadLocal<String?>() // declare thread-local variable
604â†’
605â†’fun main() = runBlocking<Unit> {
606â†’//sampleStart
607â†’    threadLocal.set("main")
608â†’    println("Pre-main, current thread: ${Thread.currentThread()}, thread local value: '${threadLocal.get()}'")
609â†’    val job = launch(Dispatchers.Default + threadLocal.asContextElement(value = "launch")) {
610â†’        println("Launch start, current thread: ${Thread.currentThread()}, thread local value: '${threadLocal.get()}'")
611â†’        yield()
612â†’        println("After yield, current thread: ${Thread.currentThread()}, thread local value: '${threadLocal.get()}'")
613â†’    }
614â†’    job.join()
615â†’    println("Post-main, current thread: ${Thread.currentThread()}, thread local value: '${threadLocal.get()}'")
616â†’//sampleEnd    
617â†’}
618â†’```  
619â†’{kotlin-runnable="true" kotlin-min-compiler-version="1.3"}
620â†’
621â†’> You can get the full code [here](../../kotlinx-coroutines-core/jvm/test/guide/example-context-11.kt).
622â†’>
623â†’{type="note"}
624â†’
625â†’In this example we launch a new coroutine in a background thread pool using [Dispatchers.Default], so
626â†’it works on a different thread from the thread pool, but it still has the value of the thread local variable
627â†’that we specified using `threadLocal.asContextElement(value = "launch")`,
628â†’no matter which thread the coroutine is executed on.
629â†’Thus, the output (with [debug](#debugging-coroutines-and-threads)) is:
630â†’
631â†’```text
632â†’Pre-main, current thread: Thread[main @coroutine#1,5,main], thread local value: 'main'
633â†’Launch start, current thread: Thread[DefaultDispatcher-worker-1 @coroutine#2,5,main], thread local value: 'launch'
634â†’After yield, current thread: Thread[DefaultDispatcher-worker-2 @coroutine#2,5,main], thread local value: 'launch'
635â†’Post-main, current thread: Thread[main @coroutine#1,5,main], thread local value: 'main'
636â†’```
637â†’
638â†’<!--- TEST FLEXIBLE_THREAD -->
639â†’
640â†’It's easy to forget to set the corresponding context element. The thread-local variable accessed from the coroutine may
641â†’then have an unexpected value, if the thread running the coroutine is different.
642â†’To avoid such situations, it is recommended to use the [ensurePresent] method
643â†’and fail-fast on improper usages.
644â†’
645â†’`ThreadLocal` has first-class support and can be used with any primitive `kotlinx.coroutines` provides.
646â†’It has one key limitation, though: when a thread-local is mutated, a new value is not propagated to the coroutine caller 
647â†’(because a context element cannot track all `ThreadLocal` object accesses), and the updated value is lost on the next suspension.
648â†’Use [withContext] to update the value of the thread-local in a coroutine, see [asContextElement] for more details. 
649â†’
650â†’Alternatively, a value can be stored in a mutable box like `class Counter(var i: Int)`, which is, in turn, 
651â†’stored in a thread-local variable. However, in this case you are fully responsible to synchronize 
652â†’potentially concurrent modifications to the variable in this mutable box.
653â†’
654â†’For advanced usage, for example for integration with logging MDC, transactional contexts or any other libraries
655â†’which internally use thread-locals for passing data, see the documentation of the [ThreadContextElement] interface 
656â†’that should be implemented. 
657â†’
658â†’<!--- MODULE kotlinx-coroutines-core -->
659â†’<!--- INDEX kotlinx.coroutines -->
